translation: By the dawn,
And the ten nights,
And the even and the odd,
And by the night as it passes.

arabic: هَلۡ فِي ذَٰلِكَ قَسَمٞ لِّذِي حِجۡرٍ
transliteration: Hal fee thalika qasamun lithee hijrin
translation: Is there in that an oath for one with understanding?
notes: For quite some time in the past, I have been thinking about the divine oaths mentioned in the first four Verses of this Surah – particularly about the ten nights. What ten nights could those be? I have not been able to get a definite, satisfactory, answer. I looked for the answers in various published commentaries, and I found them varied and based purely on conjecture. And, in this fifth Verse, Allah Almighty is posing a question to the clueless mankind. Can anyone understand the significance of the objects on which the oath is taken? It appears rather to be a divine challenge to those who claim to be endowed with deep understanding. It is as if Allah Ta’ala is telling us that our intelligence would be of no avail at all without divine Guidance!

arabic: أَلَمۡ تَرَ كَيۡفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِعَادٍ
transliteration: Alam tara kayfa faAAala rabbuka biAAadin
translation: Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with AAad
[The ancient people] of Iram, [city of] lofty-pillared buildings,

arabic: ٱلَّتِي لَمۡ يُخۡلَقۡ مِثۡلُهَا فِي ٱلۡبِلَٰدِ
transliteration: Allatee lam yukhlaq mithluha fee albiladi
translation: Like which no city had ever been created on the lands,
And [another ancient people of] Thamood who hewed the rocks in the valley [to carve out buildings there from],
And Pharaoh of the many tent pegs (i.e. with means and power),
Who committed transgression in the lands?
They often spread anarchy in them.
Then their Lord lashed them with whip of punishment.
Indeed, your Lord is ever watchful!
notes: Faamma al-insanu itha ma ibtalahu rabbuhu faakramahu wanaAAAAamahu fayaqoolu rabbee akramani

arabic: فَأَمَّا ٱلۡإِنسَٰنُ إِذَا مَا ٱبۡتَلَىٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَأَكۡرَمَهُۥ وَنَعَّمَهُۥ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّيٓ أَكۡرَمَنِ
transliteration: Faamma alinsanu itha ma ibtalahu rabbuhu faakramahu wanaAAAAamahu fayaqoolu rabbee akramani
translation: Then as his Lord tests him by bestowing honour and bounties upon him, man says, “My Lord has honoured me!”

arabic: وَأَمَّآ إِذَا مَا ٱبۡتَلَىٰهُ فَقَدَرَ عَلَيۡهِ رِزۡقَهُۥ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّيٓ أَهَٰنَنِ
transliteration: Waamma itha ma ibtalahu faqadara AAalayhi rizqahu fayaqoolu rabbee ahanani
translation: And as He tests him by restricting his provision, he says, “My Lord has humiliated me!”
No! On the contrary, you do not treat the orphan honourably,
And urge not one another to feed the poor,
And devour the inheritance – devouring it all!
And love the worldly wealth – loving it immensely.

arabic: كَلَّآۖ إِذَا دُكَّتِ ٱلۡأَرۡضُ دَكّٗا دَكّٗا
transliteration: Kalla itha dukkati alardu dakkan dakkan
translation: [And] no, [the Lord does not restrict His provisions to man to humiliate him]! When the earth is pounded to make it level,
And when your Lord reveals Himself with rows upon rows of angels,
notes: Wajee-a yawma-ithin bijahannama yawma-ithin yatathakkaru al-insanu waanna lahu alththikra

arabic: وَجِاْيٓءَ يَوۡمَئِذِۭ بِجَهَنَّمَۚ يَوۡمَئِذٖ يَتَذَكَّرُ ٱلۡإِنسَٰنُ وَأَنَّىٰ لَهُ ٱلذِّكۡرَىٰ
transliteration: Wajeea yawmaithin bijahannama yawmaithin yatathakkaru alinsanu waanna lahu alththikra
translation: And Hell brought near, that Day will man remember [what the Qur’aan had told him]! Of what avail will that remembrance be to him?
Man will say, “Would that I had sent ahead for this life of mine!”
Then, on that Day, Allah will punish him as none other can,
And bind him as none other can.
“O you contented soul!”
“Return to your Lord pleased [with yourself], pleasing (to your Lord).”
“Enter then to be among My devotees!”
“And enter My Garden!”
notes:
Verses 27 to 30 constitute the divine welcome address to those who have successfully passed the lifelong test of this world.

The Qur’aan speaks of two other sorts of souls: nafsa la-ammarah, soul prone to evil [[12:53]], and, nafsil-lawwamah, self-reproaching soul [[75:2]]. The soul, apparently, must graduate to its third stage of nafs almut-mainnah to deserve the divine welcome.
